transcript.text: Design a URL Shortener System Like TinyURL or Bitly

blocks.md:
## 1. Requirements (Functional + Non-Functional)

> 🎙️ "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock the requirements and rough scale, sketch the API and data model, draw the high-level design one requirement at a time, then go deep on the two or three hardest parts — generating unique codes, the read hot-path, and analytics — checking in with you as I go. Sound good?"

The core tension: this is a **read-heavy, write-light** system where a tiny write (shorten a URL once) is read back millions of times (every click). Almost all our engineering budget goes to making the redirect fast and never colliding on codes — not to the write path.

**Functional (the core flows — "above the line"):**

1. **Shorten a long URL into a short code.** Users POST a long URL and get back a short link like `tiny.in/aZ93Kd`.
   - 🗣️ Plain words: "Someone pastes a giant Amazon link, hits a button, and gets a tiny link they can text to a friend."
   - ⚡ Why it's hard: every code must be globally unique forever with zero collisions, generated fast, even with thousands of writes per second across many servers.

2. **Redirect a short code to the original URL.** When someone visits `tiny.in/aZ93Kd`, the system 301/302-redirects them to the original long URL.
   - 🗣️ Plain words: "You click the tiny link and your browser instantly lands on the real Amazon page — you never even notice the hop."
   - ⚡ Why it's hard: this is the firehose — billions of lookups must each return in a few milliseconds, even for links created years ago.

3. **Support custom aliases.** If a user asks for `tiny.in/my-sale`, honor it if it's free; reject it if taken.
   - 🗣️ Plain words: "A business wants `tiny.in/blackfriday` instead of random letters, and we let them have it if nobody grabbed it first."
   - ⚡ Why it's hard: custom and auto-generated codes share one namespace, so we must prevent two users from racing for the same alias.

4. **Track click analytics (optional).** Count how many times each short link is accessed, and ideally when/where.
   - 🗣️ Plain words: "The business owner can see their link got clicked 12,000 times this week."
   - ⚡ Why it's hard: we can't slow the redirect down to count — analytics must be fire-and-forget and aggregated off the hot path.

**Below the line (out of scope):** user accounts/auth dashboards, link editing after creation, paid tiers/billing, malicious-URL scanning, QR codes, A/B link routing. I'll mention where these slot in but won't design them.

**Non-Functional:**

1. **Low latency on redirect — p99 under ~50ms at the edge.**
   - 🗣️ Plain words: "A click feels instant — under a twentieth of a second even for the slowest 1% of clicks."
   - 💥 What breaks without it: every redirect adds a visible delay before the real page loads; users perceive the service as broken and stop using shortened links.

2. **High availability — 99.99% (four nines) on the redirect path.**
   - 🗣️ Plain words: "Four nines means under ~52 minutes of total downtime per year — links basically always work."
   - 💥 What breaks without it: a shortened link printed on a billboard or in an email goes dead, and there's no fallback URL — the click is simply lost.

3. **Scalability — billions of stored URLs, ~200M requests/day, growing.**
   - 🗣️ Plain words: "The system keeps working as we go from millions to billions of links without a rewrite."
   - 💥 What breaks without it: the code keyspace or a single database runs out of room/throughput and we start colliding or timing out under load.

4. **Durability / no silent loss — a created mapping is never lost or expired unless asked.**
   - 🗣️ Plain words: "Once you make a link, it works forever unless you set an expiry."
   - 💥 What breaks without it: an old link silently 404s, breaking content that's been shared widely and permanently.

5. **Uniqueness — codes never collide.**
   - 🗣️ Plain words: "No two different long URLs ever get the same short code."
   - 💥 What breaks without it: two destinations share a code and clicks land on the wrong site — a correctness and security disaster.

> 💬 "The whole game here is a cheap, collision-free write and a blisteringly fast, always-up read — so I'll spend my design budget on the redirect cache and the code generator, not on the write path."

🎙️ **Script:** "So at its heart this is four flows: shorten a URL, redirect a code, allow custom aliases, and optionally count clicks. The thing that makes it interesting isn't any single feature — it's the read-to-write ratio. We write a mapping once and read it potentially millions of times, so my non-functionals are dominated by redirect latency and availability. I want redirects under about fifty milliseconds at four-nines availability, billions of links stored durably, and absolute uniqueness on codes. Everything else I'll treat as below the line."

## 2. Clarifying Questions & Assumptions

1. **"Is 200M requests/day mostly reads (clicks) or writes (new links)?"**
   - 🗣️ Why I'm asking: "If it's read-dominated I pour everything into a cache and read replicas; if writes are heavy I have to worry about the code generator's throughput."
   - ↔️ Design fork: If READ-heavy → cache-first, read replicas, simple write path. If WRITE-heavy → distributed ID generation becomes the bottleneck I deep-dive.
   - Assumption: ~100:1 read:write — so ~200M total ≈ mostly clicks, ~2M new links/day.

2. **"How short must the code be, and what character set?"**
   - 🗣️ Why I'm asking: "Code length sets the size of my keyspace — I need to prove we won't run out for years."
   - ↔️ Design fork: If 6 chars base62 → ~56 billion codes. If 7 chars → ~3.5 trillion, much more headroom.
   - Assumption: base62 (a–z, A–Z, 0–9), 7 characters.

3. **"Do links ever expire?"**
   - 🗣️ Why I'm asking: "Expiry changes storage growth and lets me reclaim codes; no expiry means storage only grows."
   - ↔️ Design fork: If YES → TTL field + background cleanup + code recycling. If NO → append-only, storage grows forever, never recycle codes.
   - Assumption: no expiry by default, optional per-link TTL.

4. **"How fast must redirects be, and from where in the world?"**
   - 🗣️ Why I'm asking: "If users are global I need edge caching/CDN; if one region I can keep it simpler."
   - ↔️ Design fork: If GLOBAL → multi-region + CDN/edge cache. If single-region → one cache tier suffices.
   - Assumption: global users, p99 < 50ms → edge caching.

5. **"How strong must analytics be — exact counts or approximate?"**
   - 🗣️ Why I'm asking: "Exact real-time counts are expensive; approximate lets me batch and stay off the hot path."
   - ↔️ Design fork: If EXACT/real-time → synchronous increment (slows redirect). If APPROX/batched → async event stream.
   - Assumption: approximate, eventually-consistent, async.

6. **"Are custom aliases first-come-first-served, and can they collide with auto codes?"**
   - 🗣️ Why I'm asking: "This decides whether I need one namespace with a uniqueness check or two separate spaces."
   - ↔️ Design fork: If SHARED namespace → conditional insert must guard both. If SEPARATE → reserve a prefix/range for custom.
   - Assumption: shared namespace, first-come-first-served, conditional write guards collisions.

7. **"What's the read-your-own-writes expectation — must a freshly created link work immediately everywhere?"**
   - 🗣️ Why I'm asking: "Replication and cache propagation take time; if the creator clicks instantly I must handle the lag."
   - ↔️ Design fork: If MUST be instant → write-through cache + read from primary on miss. If eventual OK → relax.
   - Assumption: the creating user must see their link work immediately (write-through cache).

> 🤝 Checkpoint: "Those are my assumptions — read-heavy, 7-char base62, no default expiry, global with edge caching, async analytics. Want me to adjust any before I size it?"

## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers)

QPS — queries per second, how many requests hit the servers each second. DAU — daily active users. TTL — time-to-live, how long a cached value is kept before expiring.

| Metric | Derivation (step-by-step) | Rounded | Talkable phrase | Decision it drives |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Read QPS (clicks) | 200M req/day total, ~99% reads = ~198M; 198M ÷ 86,400s ≈ 2,292/s; peak ≈ 3x avg ≈ 6,900/s | ~7K/s | "about 7 thousand redirects a second at peak" | Must serve from cache; one DB can't take this on cold misses |
| Write QPS (new links) | ~2M new/day; 2M ÷ 86,400 ≈ 23/s; peak 3x ≈ 70/s | ~70/s | "well under a hundred writes a second" | Write path is trivial — ID generator easily keeps up |
| Read:write ratio | 198M ÷ 2M ≈ 99 | ~100:1 | "about a hundred reads per write" | Budget goes to reads/cache, not writes |
| Keyspace (7-char base62) | 62^7 = 62x62x... ≈ 3.5 trillion | ~3.5T | "three and a half trillion possible codes" | Won't exhaust for centuries at 2M/day |
| Years to exhaust | 3.5T ÷ (2M/day x 365) ≈ 4,700 years | ~4,700 yrs | "thousands of years of headroom" | 7 chars is safe; no need to recycle codes |
| Storage (5 yrs) | 2M/day x 365 x 5 ≈ 3.65B rows; ~500 bytes/row (code + long URL + meta) ≈ 1.8TB; x3 replication + index overhead ≈ ~6TB | ~6TB | "single-digit terabytes" | Storage is a non-issue — fits comfortably, shard later |
| Cache working set | Say 20% of links are "hot" and get 80% of clicks; hot set ≈ small fraction; even 100M hot mappings x ~100 bytes ≈ 10GB | ~10–50GB | "tens of gigs of hot links" | Fits in a Redis cluster easily |

The ONE number that forces a decision: **~7K redirect QPS at peak.** A single Postgres primary reading from disk tops out well below this for random key lookups, and we need p99 < 50ms globally — so the **cache (Redis + CDN edge) is mandatory**, not optional. Flip threshold: if reads dropped under ~500/s we could skip the cache and serve from DB; we're 14x past that.

🎙️ **Script:** "The number that matters is about seven thousand redirects a second at peak against only seventy writes — roughly a hundred-to-one read-to-write. So this is overwhelmingly a read system: I'll spend my budget on a cache tier and edge caching, and the write path stays dead simple. Storage and keyspace are non-issues — seven base-sixty-two characters gives three and a half trillion codes, thousands of years of runway, and only a few terabytes of data."

## 4. Core Entities

🎙️ This is a first draft of the entities — I'll add full field definitions in the data model in §7.

- **URLMapping** — the heart of the system: owns the short code → long URL relationship plus metadata (created time, owner, optional expiry).
  - 🗣️ Simple analogy: "Think of it like one row in a giant phone book — short code on the left, full destination on the right, and you look up the left to get the right."
  - ⚠️ Interviewer probe: "How do you guarantee the short code is unique?" → answer: the code is unique by construction (counter + base62 encode) or via a conditional insert that fails if the key already exists.

- **ClickEvent** — an append-only record of a single redirect: which code, when, and coarse context (region, referrer, device).
  - 🗣️ Simple analogy: "Like a turnstile tick — every time someone walks through, we drop one ticket in a bucket; we count the bucket later, not at the turnstile."
  - ⚠️ Interviewer probe: "Won't logging every click slow the redirect?" → answer: no, we fire the event asynchronously to a queue; the redirect returns before the event is processed.

- **ClickAggregate** — rolled-up counts per code per time window (e.g., clicks/day), derived from ClickEvents.
  - 🗣️ Simple analogy: "The daily tally sheet — instead of re-counting every ticket, we keep a running total per link per day."
  - ⚠️ Interviewer probe: "Exact or approximate?" → answer: eventually consistent; a few seconds of lag is fine for a dashboard.

🎙️ **Script:** "There are really three entities. The star is the URLMapping — code to long URL, that's the whole product. Then for analytics I split into raw ClickEvents, which are append-only and fire off the hot path, and ClickAggregates, the rolled-up counts a dashboard reads. The one to watch is URLMapping, because its uniqueness and read speed are the entire system. I'll flesh out the fields and stores in the data model section."

## 5. API / Interface

**1. Create short URL** — `POST /v1/urls`
- WHY POST: it creates new server state (a new mapping), so POST, not GET.
- Key request fields: `longUrl: string` — the destination we're shortening, the only required input. `customAlias: string?` — optional desired code; server rejects if taken. `expiresAt: timestamp?` — optional TTL. The client does NOT send the short code for auto-generation — the server mints it so clients can't forge or collide codes.
- Example:
```
// Request
POST /v1/urls
{ "longUrl": "https://www.amazon.com/some/very/long/product/url", "customAlias": null }

// Response 201
{ "shortUrl": "https://tiny.in/aZ93Kd", "code": "aZ93Kd", "longUrl": "https://www.amazon.com/...", "createdAt": "2026-06-19T10:00:00Z" }
```
- Key design decision: send an **idempotency key** header (or dedup on a hash of longUrl+owner) so a retried create returns the same code instead of minting a second one.

**2. Redirect** — `GET /{code}`
- WHY GET: it only reads state and triggers a redirect; safe and cacheable.
- Key request fields: just the `code` in the path. Nothing else trusted from the client.
- Example:
```
GET /aZ93Kd
// Response 301 (or 302)
Location: https://www.amazon.com/some/very/long/product/url
```
- Key design decision: **302 (temporary) vs 301 (permanent).** A 301 lets browsers cache the redirect forever — fast, but the click never reaches our server again, so **analytics die**. We use **302** so every click hits us and can be counted, accepting slightly less browser-side caching. (⚠️ Trap: defaulting to 301 silently kills analytics.)

**3. Get link stats** — `GET /v1/urls/{code}/stats`
- WHY GET: read-only analytics fetch.
- Key request fields: `code` in path; optional `from`/`to` query params for the window.
- Example:
```
GET /v1/urls/aZ93Kd/stats?from=2026-06-01&to=2026-06-19
// Response 200
{ "code": "aZ93Kd", "totalClicks": 12840, "byDay": [{ "date": "2026-06-18", "clicks": 2103 }] }
```
- Key design decision: served from the **ClickAggregate** store, never by scanning raw events — so the dashboard is fast and the hot path stays untouched.

SECURITY: the caller is identified from a session/JWT (JSON Web Token — a signed token proving who you are) for create/stats; we never trust a client-supplied owner id, and the long URL is validated/normalized server-side to avoid open-redirect abuse.

> 💬 "I default to REST and a 302 on the redirect specifically so I keep analytics — that's the non-obvious call here."

🎙️ **Script:** "Three endpoints. POST slash urls creates a mapping — client sends the long URL and optionally a custom alias, server mints the code and dedups on retries with an idempotency key. GET slash code is the redirect, and I deliberately use a 302 not a 301 so every click still reaches us for analytics. And GET stats reads pre-aggregated counts so the dashboard never touches the hot path."

## 6. High-Level Design (built one functional requirement at a time)

Color palette: blue = clients, green = our services, amber = async/queues, grey = datastores, purple = external/CDN.

### 6.1 "Shorten a long URL into a short code"

The user posts a long URL; we mint a globally unique code, store the mapping, and return the short link. The interesting part is generating a unique code without a per-write collision check.

**ARCHITECTURE DECISIONS TABLE**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Write API service | A stateless service that accepts create requests and writes the mapping | Simple, horizontally scalable; ~70 writes/s is trivial | Not a monolith bundling reads — read path scales differently | Another service to deploy |
| ID/Counter (KGS) | Key Generation Service — hands out unique numeric IDs in ranges, base62-encoded to a code | Unique BY CONSTRUCTION, no DB collision check per write | Not random codes (need a DB check every write to avoid collision); not URL-hash (hashes collide) | A central allocator to make HA |
| Primary DB | Stores code → long URL durably (key-value style) | Need durable, indexed lookups by code at scale | Not in-memory only (not durable); not a graph DB (overkill) | Must shard as it grows |

🎙️ NARRATION: "For the write service I keep it stateless and separate from reads so each scales on its own — writes are tiny. For the code itself I use a Key Generation Service, the KGS: it hands out numeric ID ranges that I base62-encode into the short code, so codes are unique by construction and I never have to check the database on every write to avoid a collision. I considered random codes — but those need a uniqueness check on every single write — and hashing the URL, but hashes collide. The trade-off is the KGS is a central allocator, so I'll make it highly available with replicas and pre-fetched ranges."

**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **KGS (Key Generation Service)** — a small service that pre-generates or hands out unique numeric IDs in blocks. Here we use it so each new link gets a guaranteed-unique number we encode into a code, avoiding per-write collision checks.
> **base62** — encoding a number using 62 symbols (a–z, A–Z, 0–9) instead of 10 digits. Here it turns a long numeric ID into a short 6–7 character code.
> **Stateless service** — a server that keeps no per-request memory, so any instance can handle any request. Here it lets us add write servers freely behind a load balancer.

**Create flow**
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Client[Client app]
    Client -->|"1: POST longUrl"| API[Write API svc]
    subgraph WritePath["Write path"]
        API -->|"2: get next id range"| KGS[KGS / ID svc]
        API -->|"3: base62 encode"| API
    end
    API -->|"4: insert code to url"| DB[(Primary DB)]
    KGS -.->|"0: prefetch ranges"| DB
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
    class Client client
    class API,KGS svc
    class DB db
```
↳ reuses existing: none — first slice.

**NUMBERED STEP NARRATION**
1. Client POSTs the long URL to the Write API. The API owns validation and orchestration because it's the single entry point for creates. State: a request is in flight, nothing persisted yet. What if invalid URL → reject with 400 before touching the DB.
2. API asks the KGS for the next unique numeric ID (from a pre-fetched in-memory range). KGS owns uniqueness so no two writes ever get the same number. State: one ID is now consumed. What if KGS range is exhausted → it pre-fetches the next range from the DB before running dry.
3. API base62-encodes the number into a 7-char code locally — cheap CPU, no I/O. State: code computed. What if a custom alias was requested → skip KGS, use a conditional insert instead (6.3).
4. API inserts the code → long URL row. State: mapping is durable. What if the insert races a duplicate (custom alias) → conditional insert fails, we return 409.

> 💬 "The trick is the counter: codes are unique because the number is unique — I never scan the DB to check for collisions."

🎙️ **Script:** "To create a link, the write service grabs a unique number from a Key Generation Service — that's a tiny service that hands out ID ranges — base62-encodes it into a short code, and stores the mapping. The whole reason for the counter is that it makes codes unique by construction, so I avoid a collision check on every write. The trade-off is a central allocator, which I'll make highly available."

### 6.2 "Redirect a short code to the original URL"

This is the firehose — ~7K/s. The user hits a code; we look up the long URL and 302-redirect. The whole interest is serving this from memory, not disk.

**ARCHITECTURE DECISIONS TABLE**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| CDN / edge cache | Servers near the user that cache the redirect response | Cuts global latency to ~10–20ms; absorbs most reads | Not origin-only (adds 100ms+ of round-trip for far users) | Edge can briefly serve a stale mapping |
| Redis cache | In-memory key-value store holding code → long URL | A Postgres disk lookup can't do 7K/s at <50ms; Redis does 100K+/s | Not DB-only (too slow, melts under load) | Cache can miss; needs warming |
| Read replicas | Read-only copies of the primary DB | Serve cache misses without hitting the write primary | Not single primary for reads (couples read load to writes) | Slight replica lag |

🎙️ NARRATION: "The redirect path is all about memory. I put a CDN at the edge so most clicks never even reach my servers, and behind it a Redis cache — an in-memory key-value store — because a Postgres disk read simply can't do seven thousand random lookups a second under fifty milliseconds. On a cache miss I read from a replica, not the primary, so reads never compete with writes. The trade-off is staleness — an edge or cache entry can be briefly out of date — but mappings rarely change, so that's fine."

**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **CDN (Content Delivery Network)** — a global network of edge servers close to users. Here it caches the redirect so a click in Tokyo is answered in Tokyo, not from a US origin.
> **Redis** — an in-memory key-value store, extremely fast because data lives in RAM. Here it holds hot code→URL mappings so redirects skip the database.
> **Read replica** — a read-only copy of the database kept in sync with the primary. Here it absorbs cache-miss reads so the write primary stays free.
> **Cache hit ratio** — the fraction of reads answered from cache. Here a ~95% hit ratio means only ~5% of clicks ever reach the DB.

**Redirect flow**
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Client[Client app]
    Client -->|"1: GET code"| CDN[CDN edge]
    subgraph ReadPath["Read hot path"]
        CDN -->|"2: miss to origin"| RSVC[Redirect svc]
        RSVC -->|"3: lookup code"| Redis[(Redis cache)]
        Redis -.->|"4: miss"| Replica[(Read replica)]
    end
    Replica -.->|"5: backfill"| Redis
    Client -->|"6: 302 to longUrl"| Client
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
    classDef ext fill:#3a2a5c,stroke:#6B46C1,color:#e6edf3
    class Client client
    class RSVC svc
    class Redis,Replica db
    class CDN ext
```
↳ reuses existing: Primary DB / replica (from §6.1).

**NUMBERED STEP NARRATION**
1. Client requests `GET /{code}`, hitting the nearest CDN edge. CDN owns edge caching to minimize latency. State: none changed. What if cold edge → forwards to origin.
2. On an edge miss, the request goes to the Redirect service. It owns the read orchestration. State: none. What if origin overloaded → CDN can serve stale-while-revalidate.
3. Redirect service looks up the code in Redis — a single in-memory GET. State: none. What if Redis down → fall through to replica (degraded but working).
4. On a Redis miss, read from a read replica. State: none. What if not found anywhere → return 404.
5. Backfill Redis with the result (with a TTL) so the next click is a hit. State: cache now warm for this code.
6. Return a 302 with the Location header; browser follows to the long URL. State: redirect served; a ClickEvent is fired async (6.4). What if the long URL is malformed → we validated it at create time, so this is safe.

> 💬 "95% cache hit means Postgres only sees ~350 reads a second instead of 7,000 — that's the whole point of the cache."

🎙️ **Script:** "A redirect tries the CDN edge first, then Redis, then a read replica, backfilling the cache on the way back. At a ninety-five percent hit ratio the database only ever sees a few hundred reads a second instead of seven thousand. I return a 302 rather than a 301 so the click still reaches us for analytics. The trade-off is occasional staleness, which is fine because mappings almost never change."

### 6.3 "Support custom aliases"

A user requests a specific code. This mostly reuses the write path but adds a conditional insert to guard the shared namespace.

↳ builds on §6.1 diagram — no new components; the new behavior is a **conditional insert** ("INSERT ... IF NOT EXISTS") on the code, instead of pulling from the KGS.

**NUMBERED STEP NARRATION**
1. Client POSTs `longUrl` + `customAlias`. State: in flight. What if alias blank → fall back to KGS path.
2. API attempts a conditional insert keyed on the alias — succeeds only if the code is free. The DB's atomic write owns the uniqueness guarantee, so two users racing for `tiny.in/sale` can't both win — exactly one insert succeeds, the other gets a 409. State: alias now reserved (or rejected). What if it collides with a future auto-generated code → we offset the KGS counter range so auto codes never overlap the custom space (or reserve a length/prefix), eliminating cross-namespace collisions.

> 💬 "One atomic conditional insert is the whole collision story — the database, not my app code, decides the winner."

🎙️ **Script:** "Custom aliases reuse the write path but swap the counter for a conditional insert — insert only if the code isn't taken. That makes the database the referee when two people race for the same alias: exactly one wins, the other gets a clean 409. To stop a custom alias from ever clashing with a future auto-generated code, I keep the two in separate ranges of the keyspace."

### 6.4 "Track click analytics"

Every redirect should be counted without slowing it down. New component: an async event pipeline.

**ARCHITECTURE DECISIONS TABLE**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Message queue (Kafka) | A durable log that buffers a firehose of events | Decouples counting from redirecting; absorbs 7K events/s | Not synchronous DB increment (adds latency to every redirect) | Counts are eventually consistent |
| Stream processor | A worker that consumes events and rolls up counts | Aggregates off the hot path | Not counting in the redirect svc (couples concerns) | A few seconds of lag |
| Analytics store | A column/OLAP store for aggregates | Fast range/time queries for dashboards | Not the primary OLTP DB (analytics scans would hurt redirects) | Separate store to maintain |

🎙️ NARRATION: "Analytics has to be fire-and-forget. The redirect service drops a click event onto Kafka — a durable log that buffers a firehose so nothing's lost if the consumer is slow — and returns immediately. A stream processor reads the log and rolls counts into an analytics store the dashboard queries. I rejected incrementing a counter synchronously inside the redirect because that would put a database write on the hot path of every click. The trade-off is counts lag by a few seconds, which nobody cares about on a dashboard."

**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **Kafka** — a durable, replayable log that buffers events. Here it holds click events so analytics can catch up without slowing redirects.
> **Stream processor** — a worker that continuously consumes events and computes rollups. Here it turns raw clicks into per-link daily counts.
> **OLAP store** — a database optimized for aggregations over many rows. Here it serves dashboard queries fast without touching the redirect path.

**Analytics flow**
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RSVC[Redirect svc]
    RSVC -.->|"1: emit click event"| Kafka[[Kafka log]]
    subgraph AsyncPath["Async analytics path"]
        Kafka -.->|"2: consume"| Worker[Stream processor]
        Worker -.->|"3: rollup counts"| OLAP[(Analytics store)]
    end
    OLAP -->|"4: read stats"| StatsAPI[Stats API]
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
    classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
    class RSVC,StatsAPI svc
    class Kafka queue
    class Worker svc
    class OLAP db
```
↳ reuses existing: Redirect svc (from §6.2).

**NUMBERED STEP NARRATION**
1. Redirect service emits a click event to Kafka — non-blocking, fire-and-forget. State: event durably buffered. What if Kafka is down → buffer locally / drop with a metric; the redirect itself still succeeds.
2. Stream processor consumes events. State: events being aggregated. What if the worker crashes → Kafka replays from the last committed offset, at-least-once.
3. Worker rolls up counts (idempotently, keyed by event id to avoid double-counting on replay). State: aggregates updated. What if duplicate event → dedup by event id.
4. Stats API serves the aggregate. State: none. What if window has no data → return zeros.

> 💬 "The redirect never waits on analytics — it drops one event on the log and moves on; counting happens later."

🎙️ **Script:** "For analytics I keep the redirect fast by firing a click event onto Kafka and returning immediately. A stream processor reads that log and rolls counts into an analytics store the dashboard reads. Because Kafka is durable and replayable I get at-least-once delivery, and I make the rollup idempotent by event id so replays don't double-count. The cost is a few seconds of lag, which is invisible on a dashboard."

**Final (high-level)** — the reconciled union of all slices:
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Client[Client app]
    Client -->|"1: GET code"| CDN[CDN edge]
    Client -->|"w1: POST longUrl"| API[Write API svc]
    subgraph ReadPath["Read hot path"]
        CDN -->|"2: miss"| RSVC[Redirect svc]
        RSVC -->|"3: lookup"| Redis[(Redis cache)]
    end
    subgraph WritePath["Write / create path"]
        API -->|"w2: next id"| KGS[KGS / ID svc]
        API -->|"w3: insert"| DB[(Primary DB)]
    end
    subgraph AsyncPath["Async analytics"]
        RSVC -.->|"4: click event"| Kafka[[Kafka log]]
        Kafka -.->|"5: consume"| Worker[Stream processor]
        Worker -.->|"6: rollup"| OLAP[(Analytics store)]
    end
    Redis -.->|"3b: miss"| Replica[(Read replica)]
    DB -->|"repl"| Replica
    Replica -.->|"backfill"| Redis
    OLAP -->|"stats"| StatsAPI[Stats API]
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
    classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
    classDef ext fill:#3a2a5c,stroke:#6B46C1,color:#e6edf3
    class Client client
    class RSVC,API,KGS,Worker,StatsAPI svc
    class Kafka queue
    class Redis,DB,Replica,OLAP db
    class CDN ext
```
Schema notes: Primary DB key = `code` (PK, shard key); URLMapping holds `code, long_url, owner_id, created_at, expires_at`. Analytics store keyed by `(code, day)`.

🎙️ **Script:** "Read path: a click hits the CDN, then Redis, then a replica on a miss — that's the seven-thousand-per-second firehose, served almost entirely from memory. Write path: the API pulls a unique id from the KGS, encodes it, and inserts into the primary DB — only tens of writes a second. Async path: every redirect fires a click event onto Kafka, a stream processor rolls it up into an analytics store the dashboard reads — completely off the hot path."

> 🤝 Checkpoint: "That's the skeleton end to end. Want me deeper on the unique-code generation, the read hot-path and cache, or the analytics pipeline?"

## 7. Data Model & Storage

**PART A — ENTITY TABLE**

| Entity | Key fields (type) | Chosen store | Shard/partition key | Consistency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| URLMapping | `code: string (PK)`, `long_url: text`, `owner_id: string`, `created_at: ts`, `expires_at: ts?` | Key-value DB (Cassandra/DynamoDB) or sharded Postgres | `code` | Strong on write (conditional insert), eventual on cached read |
| ClickEvent | `event_id: uuid (PK)`, `code: string`, `ts: ts`, `region: string`, `referrer: string` | Kafka → OLAP | `code` | At-least-once |
| ClickAggregate | `code: string`, `day: date`, `clicks: bigint` (PK = code+day) | OLAP / columnar | `code` | Eventual |

**PART B — STORAGE DECISION CARDS**

**🗄️ Key-value store (Cassandra/DynamoDB) — used for: URLMapping**
1. What it is (plain words): A distributed key-value database that stores rows looked up by a single key and spreads them across many machines automatically.
2. Why we picked it — the access pattern: Our only read pattern is "given a code, get the long URL" — a pure point lookup at billions of rows and ~7K/s. A key-value store nails exactly that and scales horizontally by the code key with no joins.
3. What we considered instead and rejected:
   - **Single Postgres** — fine at first, but billions of rows and 7K/s point reads force sharding anyway; we'd be hand-building what a KV store gives natively.
   - **MongoDB** — works, but we don't need documents or rich queries — just a key lookup; KV is simpler and cheaper at this scale.
   - **Pure in-memory (Redis only)** — not durable; a restart loses every mapping.
4. Trade-off we accept: no rich queries/joins (we don't need them); we manage a distributed store. For custom aliases we rely on conditional writes (DynamoDB conditional put / Cassandra LWT).
5. 🗣️ How to say it: "For the mappings I'd use a key-value store like DynamoDB or Cassandra, because my only access pattern is code-to-URL point lookups at billions of rows. I looked at a single Postgres, but I'd have to shard it manually anyway, and I don't need joins. The catch is I rely on conditional writes for custom-alias uniqueness, which both support."

**🗄️ Redis — used for: hot code→URL cache**
1. What it is: An in-memory key-value store; data lives in RAM so reads are microseconds.
2. Why we picked it: 7K/s redirects at p99<50ms can't come off disk; Redis serves 100K+ ops/s easily and holds the ~tens-of-GB hot set.
3. Considered & rejected: **Memcached** — fine, but Redis gives persistence options and richer ops; **local in-process cache** — doesn't share across servers, low hit ratio.
4. Trade-off: cache can go stale or cold; we warm it via backfill and accept brief staleness.
5. 🗣️ How to say it: "Redis sits in front of the DB because redirects have to be memory-fast; I backfill on miss and accept a little staleness since mappings rarely change."

**🗄️ Kafka + OLAP — used for: ClickEvent / ClickAggregate**
1. What it is: Kafka is a durable event log; the OLAP store is a columnar DB optimized for aggregations.
2. Why we picked it: counting must be off the hot path and survive consumer slowness; Kafka buffers, the OLAP store answers time-range dashboard queries fast.
3. Considered & rejected: **Synchronous counter in primary DB** — puts a write on every redirect, kills latency; **counting in Redis only** — not durable for historical analytics.
4. Trade-off: eventual consistency on counts; extra infra.
5. 🗣️ How to say it: "Clicks go onto Kafka and get rolled up into an analytics store, so the redirect never waits on counting — the price is counts lag a few seconds."

**PART C — Per-operation consistency summary**

| Operation | Store | Consistency level | Why right here |
|---|---|---|---|
| Create custom alias | KV DB | Strong (conditional write) | Two users must not both win the same alias |
| Create auto code | KV DB | Strong (unique by KGS) | Code unique by construction |
| Redirect read | Redis → replica | Eventual (cached) | Mappings rarely change; speed > freshness |
| Read-your-own-write | Write-through cache + primary read | Strong for creator | Creator must see their link instantly |
| Click count | Kafka → OLAP | Eventual | A few seconds lag is invisible |

🎙️ **Script:** "The dominant store is a key-value DB for the mappings, because the only access pattern is a point lookup by code at billions of rows. In front of it is Redis for the memory-fast redirect, and behind the redirect is a Kafka-plus-OLAP pipeline for analytics. I considered a single Postgres for everything but rejected it because I'd shard it manually anyway and I never need joins. Writes that matter — custom aliases — are strongly consistent via conditional writes; reads and counts are happily eventual."

## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great

🆘 If you get stuck: go back to the one binding constraint — "unique code, served from memory" — and reason forward from there.

### How do we generate unique short codes at scale?

**#### Bad: Random code + DB collision check**
🗣️ What this is in plain words: "Roll random letters, then ask the database if that code already exists; if it does, roll again."
Approach: generate a random 7-char base62 string, do a `SELECT` to check existence, insert if free.
Why people try this: it's the obvious first instinct — random feels unique.
⚠️ What breaks: every write now needs a read-before-write round trip, and as the keyspace fills, collisions and retries climb. Under concurrent writes two requests can both check-then-insert the same code (a race) unless you add locking, which serializes writes.
🔁 What forces the upgrade: any real write concurrency makes the check-then-insert race unsafe and slow.

**#### Good: Hash the URL (MD5/SHA) + take first 7 chars**
↩️ What the previous tier got wrong: random needed a DB check every write; hashing is deterministic so the same URL maps to the same code with no counter.
🗣️ What this is in plain words: "Run the long URL through a fixing function that always gives the same fingerprint, and use the first few characters as the code."
Approach: `base62(MD5(longUrl))[:7]`. Deterministic, no central allocator.
Why people try this: dedup is free — same URL gives same code — and it's stateless.
⚠️ What breaks: truncated hashes **collide** — two different URLs can produce the same 7 chars (birthday problem), so you still need a collision check and a tiebreaker. And two users shortening the same URL get the same code even if they wanted separate analytics.
🔁 What forces the upgrade: collisions and shared-code analytics make this unsafe for a uniqueness-critical system.
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    U[longUrl] -->|"hash"| H[MD5]
    H -->|"base62 first 7"| C[code]
    C -.->|"collision check"| DB[(DB)]
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
    class H,C svc
    class DB db
```

**#### Great: Counter + base62 via a Key Generation Service (KGS)**
↩️ What the previous tier got wrong: hashing collides and forces a check; a monotonic counter is unique by construction — zero checks.
🗣️ What this is in plain words: "Give every new link the next number in line — 1, 2, 3 … — and write that number in a compact 62-symbol alphabet; numbers never repeat, so codes never collide."
Approach: a KGS hands out **ID ranges** (e.g., blocks of 10,000) to each write server, which it consumes from memory and base62-encodes. To avoid predictable/sequential codes, XOR or permute the id before encoding. Each server pre-fetches the next range before exhausting the current one.
🔢 Decision-forcing math: 62^7 ≈ 3.5 trillion codes ÷ 2M/day ≈ 4,700 years of runway — sequential ids never run out. Range size 10K means the KGS is consulted only once per 10K writes → ~1 KGS call every few minutes at our write rate.
Why this is great: unique by construction, no per-write DB check, KGS off the hot path, and range pre-fetch means a KGS blip doesn't stall writes.
✅ Failure matrix:
| Failure | Impact | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| KGS down briefly | Writes continue from pre-fetched range | Pre-fetch next range early |
| Server crashes mid-range | Unused ids in that range are lost (gaps) | Harmless — keyspace is huge |
| Two servers same range | Duplicate codes | KGS allocates ranges atomically; never overlaps |
| Custom alias clashes auto code | Collision | Reserve separate id ranges / prefix for custom |
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Write svc A]
    S2[Write svc B]
    S1 -->|"range 1-10k"| KGS[KGS]
    S2 -->|"range 10k-20k"| KGS
    KGS -->|"persist high-water"| DB[(KGS DB)]
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
    class S1,S2,KGS svc
    class DB db
```
🎙️ **Script:** "I generate codes with a counter, not randomness or hashing. A Key Generation Service hands each write server a block of ten thousand ids that it base62-encodes locally, so codes are unique by construction — no database collision check ever. I permute the id before encoding so codes don't look sequential. Seven characters give three and a half trillion codes, thousands of years of runway, and because servers pre-fetch ranges, a brief KGS outage doesn't stall a single write."
🧠 If they ask "why a counter, not random codes?": "Random needs a DB check on every write to avoid collisions; a counter is unique by construction. Hashing the URL also works but can collide on a truncated hash — the counter is the cleanest."

### How do we make redirects fast and always available (caching strategy)?

**#### Good: Single Redis cache in front of the DB**
🗣️ Plain words: "Keep the hottest links in fast memory so most clicks skip the database."
Approach: cache-aside — on read, check Redis; on miss, read DB and backfill with a TTL.
⚠️ What breaks: one Redis node tops out ~100–150K ops/s and is a single point of failure; a cold cache after restart sends a thundering herd to the DB.
🔁 What forces the upgrade: a node failure or a region far from the cache breaks latency/availability.

**#### Great: CDN edge + sharded Redis cluster + replicas**
↩️ What the previous tier got wrong: a single cache node is a SPOF and far from global users; we add edge caching and shard the cache.
🗣️ Plain words: "Cache the redirect at servers near each user, and split the in-memory cache across many nodes so no one node is a bottleneck."
Approach: 302 responses cached at the CDN edge (short TTL); behind it a Redis cluster sharded by code with replicas; DB read replicas absorb misses. Use single-flight (collapse duplicate concurrent misses for the same code into one DB read) to prevent a thundering herd.
🔢 Decision-forcing math: 7K/s x 95% hit = ~350/s reach the DB; spread over 3 replicas ≈ 120/s each — trivial. Cold cache (0% hit) = full 7K/s to DB → would need many replicas, so we pre-warm hot codes.
✅ Failure matrix:
| Failure | Impact | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Redis node down | Misses spike for its shard | Replica promoted; DB replicas absorb |
| Cold cache | Thundering herd to DB | Single-flight + pre-warm top codes |
| CDN edge stale | Briefly old mapping | Short TTL; mappings rarely change |
| DB replica lag | New link not visible | Read-your-own-write via primary |
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Client[Client]
    Client -->|"GET code"| CDN[CDN edge]
    CDN -->|"miss"| RC[Redis cluster]
    RC -.->|"miss"| Rep[(Replicas)]
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
    classDef ext fill:#3a2a5c,stroke:#6B46C1,color:#e6edf3
    class Client client
    class RC svc
    class Rep db
    class CDN ext
```
🎙️ **Script:** "Redirects are served from memory in layers — CDN edge first, then a sharded Redis cluster, then read replicas on a miss. At a ninety-five percent hit ratio the database only sees a few hundred reads a second. The two things I guard against are a cold cache and a thundering herd, so I pre-warm the hottest codes and collapse duplicate concurrent misses with single-flight."
🧠 If they ask "what about a cold cache?": "Worst case all 7K/s hit the DB, so I pre-warm top codes and use single-flight so a million simultaneous misses for one hot code become one DB read."

### How do we shard and replicate the database?

**#### Great: Hash-shard by code + async replicas**
🗣️ Plain words: "Split the data across many machines by the code, and keep read-only copies for speed and safety."
Approach: partition by hash of `code` so load spreads evenly (codes are uniformly distributed by construction); each shard has a primary + async read replicas. Writes go to the shard primary; reads prefer replicas/cache.
🔢 Decision-forcing math: ~6TB over, say, 6 shards = 1TB each; 70 writes/s total ≈ ~12/shard — tiny. Reads handled by cache + replicas.
⚠️ Failure modes & matrix:
| Failure | Impact | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Shard primary down | Writes to that shard stall | Promote a replica (seconds) |
| Replica lag | Stale reads | Read-your-own-write from primary |
| Hot shard | Uneven load | Hash sharding keeps codes uniform |
🎙️ **Script:** "I shard by a hash of the code so load is perfectly even — codes are uniform by construction — and give each shard a primary plus async replicas. Writes are tiny so a single primary per shard is plenty; reads come from cache and replicas. If a primary dies I promote a replica in seconds."
🧠 If they ask "won't some links be hot?": "Hot links are absorbed by the cache, not the DB — the shard only sees the ~5% of misses, spread evenly by hash."

### How do we keep analytics off the hot path (async processing + CDN)?

**#### Great: Fire-and-forget to Kafka + idempotent rollup**
🗣️ Plain words: "Drop a note about each click into a durable inbox and tally them later, instead of counting during the click."
Approach: redirect emits a click event to Kafka; a stream processor consumes, dedups by event_id, and rolls into the OLAP store. CDN absorbs most reads globally so origin load (and event volume) is bounded.
🔢 Decision-forcing math: 7K events/s x ~100 bytes = ~0.7 MB/s — trivial for Kafka. Rollups every few seconds.
✅ Failure matrix:
| Failure | Impact | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Kafka down | Events buffered/dropped | Redirect still works; local buffer + metric |
| Worker crash | Lag, no loss | Replay from last offset (at-least-once) |
| Duplicate event on replay | Over-count | Idempotent rollup by event_id |
🎙️ **Script:** "Analytics is fully decoupled — the redirect fires a click event onto Kafka and returns, and a stream processor rolls counts into an OLAP store. Kafka's durability gives me at-least-once delivery, and I make the rollup idempotent by event id so replays don't over-count. If the whole analytics pipeline is down, redirects don't even notice."
🧠 If they ask "exactly-once?": "I do at-least-once delivery plus idempotent aggregation by event id, which gives effectively-once counts without the cost of true exactly-once."

## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost

**9A — Availability targets (what the "nines" mean):**
Redirect (read) path targets **four nines = 99.99% ≈ under 52 minutes of downtime per year total.** The create (write) path targets **three nines ≈ 8.7 hours/year** — a brief inability to create new links is far less damaging than redirects failing. Mechanisms: CDN edge + sharded Redis + DB read replicas across **multi-AZ** (multiple data centres in the same region, so if one building loses power the others take over automatically) on the read path; the KGS and write DB have standby replicas.

**9B — Per-dependency failure table:**
| Component | What breaks if down | Graceful degradation (what the user sees) | Recovery action |
|---|---|---|---|
| CDN edge | Higher latency | Clicks still work, just routed to origin — a bit slower | Edge self-heals; origin scales |
| Redis cache | Misses spike | Redirects still work via DB replicas, slightly slower | Promote replica, re-warm cache |
| Read replica | Less read capacity | Cache absorbs; minor latency rise | Re-route to healthy replicas |
| Primary DB shard | That shard's writes stall | New links to that shard fail briefly; redirects unaffected (cache) | Promote replica to primary |
| KGS | New auto-codes pause | Servers use pre-fetched ranges; custom aliases still work | Restart; resume range allocation |
| Kafka/analytics | No new counts | Redirects unaffected; dashboard counts lag | Replay backlog from log |

**9C — RPO / RTO per data class:**
RPO (Recovery Point Objective) = how much data we're OK losing if it crashes right now. RPO≈0 means we lose nothing because every write is replicated before we acknowledge. RTO (Recovery Time Objective) = how long until the service works again after a crash.
| Data class | RPO | RTO | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| URLMapping (the mappings) | ≈0 | seconds–minutes | Synchronous replication / quorum write before ack |
| Click aggregates | minutes | minutes | Rebuildable by replaying Kafka |
| Click raw events | seconds | minutes | Kafka retention + replay |

**9D — Cost breakdown:**
Top 3 drivers: (1) **CDN egress** — every redirect response served globally is billed per request/byte; at ~200M/day this is the biggest line item, not the DB. (2) **Cache cluster RAM** — tens of GB of always-on Redis across replicas. (3) **Storage + replication** — a few TB x replication, modest. Rough order of magnitude: low tens of thousands of dollars/month, CDN-dominated. Biggest single optimization: **longer CDN/edge TTLs on redirects** (mappings rarely change) to cut origin and request volume — but that trades against analytics freshness, so we tune TTL carefully.

🎙️ **Script:** "When Redis dies, redirects keep working off the database replicas — just a touch slower — so users barely notice. If a write shard fails, new links to it pause for a few seconds while we promote a replica, but every existing redirect keeps flowing from cache. The only thing a user truly loses in a bad outage is a few seconds of click counts, never a working link. And the surprising cost driver is CDN egress, not the database."

## 10. Trade-off Ledger

**🔀 Decision: Counter+KGS vs hashing/random for codes**
1. What we chose and why: KGS counter + base62 — unique by construction, no per-write check.
2. What we gave up: a central allocator we must keep HA; codes aren't content-derived so the same URL can get two codes.
3. 🗣️ Plain words: "Like handing out numbered tickets at a deli instead of guessing a free number each time."
4. When this reverses: if we needed automatic dedup of identical URLs as a feature, hashing becomes attractive; if writes were truly massive and globally partitioned, we'd switch to per-region ID prefixes.
5. 🗣️ How to say it: "I use a counter because it's collision-free by design; I'd only switch to hashing if dedup of identical URLs were a product requirement."

**🔀 Decision: 302 (temporary) vs 301 (permanent) redirect**
1. What we chose and why: 302, so every click reaches us and analytics survive.
2. What we gave up: browsers re-ask us each time instead of caching the redirect — slightly more load.
3. 🗣️ Plain words: "We take a small speed hit so we can keep counting the clicks."
4. When this reverses: if analytics were dropped from scope, 301 + aggressive browser caching is faster and cheaper.
5. 🗣️ How to say it: "302 keeps analytics alive; if we didn't care about counts I'd use 301 and let browsers cache it."

**🔀 Decision: Async (eventual) analytics vs synchronous counting**
1. What we chose and why: async via Kafka — keeps the redirect fast.
2. What we gave up: real-time exact counts; we have a few seconds of lag.
3. 🗣️ Plain words: "Tally the tickets after the rush, not at the turnstile."
4. When this reverses: if a customer needed real-time exact counts (e.g., billing per click), we'd add a synchronous counter with sharded atomic increments.
5. 🗣️ How to say it: "Counting is async so redirects stay fast; I'd only go synchronous if exact real-time counts drove revenue."

**🔀 Decision: Key-value store vs single relational DB**
1. What we chose and why: KV store — our only access pattern is a point lookup at billions of rows.
2. What we gave up: rich queries/joins and easy ad-hoc reporting on the mapping table.
3. 🗣️ Plain words: "A giant, fast phone book instead of a full spreadsheet we rarely need to slice."
4. When this reverses: if the product grew rich relational features (folders, teams, complex queries over links), a relational DB (sharded) becomes worth it.
5. 🗣️ How to say it: "KV because it's pure key lookups at huge scale; I'd revisit relational only if we needed real query power over links."

🎙️ **Script:** "The decisions I'm least dogmatic about: the counter over hashing — I'd flip if dedup were a feature; the 302 over 301 — I'd flip if analytics left scope; and async over synchronous analytics — I'd flip if exact real-time counts drove money. Each ties straight back to my non-functionals: uniqueness, redirect speed, and availability."

## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers

**❓ "Two users request the same custom alias at the same time — who wins?"**
The mechanism: both attempt a conditional insert (insert-if-not-exists) on the alias key; the database serializes them so exactly one succeeds and the other gets a 409. The DB's atomic write — not application code — is the referee.
🗣️ In plain words: "The database only lets the first one in; the second is politely told it's taken."
💬 One-liner: "Exactly one wins because the conditional insert is atomic — the loser gets a clean 409."

**❓ "What happens when Redis crashes?"**
The mechanism: redirects fall through to DB read replicas — slower (single-digit-ms to ~20ms more) but fully functional; meanwhile a Redis replica is promoted and the cache re-warms. Redirects degrade, they don't fail.
🗣️ In plain words: "Links still work, just a little slower, until the cache comes back."
💬 One-liner: "Redis is an accelerator, not a dependency — we serve from replicas and re-warm."

**❓ "How do you handle a viral link doing millions of clicks (hot key)?"**
The mechanism: a hot code is cached at the CDN edge and in Redis, so nearly 100% of its clicks never reach the DB; single-flight collapses concurrent misses into one origin read. The hotter the link, the better cached it is.
🗣️ In plain words: "Popular links get cached everywhere, so popularity actually makes them cheaper to serve."
💬 One-liner: "Hot keys live at the edge — virality is a cache win, not a DB problem."

**❓ "Can the same code ever point to two different URLs (consistency)?"**
The mechanism: codes are unique by construction (KGS counter) or by atomic conditional insert (custom), and mappings are immutable after creation, so a code maps to exactly one URL forever. Cache staleness can only ever serve the one correct value, never a wrong one.
🗣️ In plain words: "Every code has one and only one destination, period."
💬 One-liner: "Uniqueness is guaranteed at write time, so a code never forks."

**❓ "How do you stop someone forging a different user's links or an open-redirect attack?"**
The mechanism: create/stats require a JWT and we derive the owner from it, never from the request body; long URLs are validated/normalized at create time and dangerous schemes blocked, so the redirect can't be weaponized into an open redirect to malware.
🗣️ In plain words: "We trust who you are from your login token, not from what you type, and we sanitize the destination."
💬 One-liner: "Identity comes from the token, the URL is sanitized at write — no forging, no open redirect."

**❓ "What's the most expensive part, and how would you cut cost?"**
The mechanism: CDN egress dominates — every one of ~200M daily redirects is billed at the edge — far more than the few-TB database. The biggest lever is longer edge/redirect TTLs (mappings rarely change) to cut request volume, traded carefully against analytics freshness.
🗣️ In plain words: "Serving the redirects to the world costs more than storing them; caching them longer is the savings."
💬 One-liner: "CDN egress is the bill; longer TTLs are the discount."

**❓ "How would you add link expiry or rate-limiting later (extensibility)?"**
The mechanism: expiry is an `expires_at` column already in the schema — a background job sweeps expired rows and the redirect checks the field on read; rate-limiting slots in at the API gateway with a per-user token bucket, no core change. The design leaves clean seams for both.
🗣️ In plain words: "We left room for it — expiry is one field and a cleanup job; rate limits sit at the front door."
💬 One-liner: "Both are additive — an expiry field plus a sweeper, and a gateway rate limiter."

🎙️ **60-second verbal summary:** "A URL shortener is a service that turns a long link into a tiny code and instantly redirects clicks back to the original. The design splits into two halves. The read half is the firehose — about seven thousand redirects a second served almost entirely from a CDN edge and a Redis cache, with database replicas only catching the five percent of misses, so a click is answered in milliseconds anywhere in the world. The write half is tiny but correctness-critical — only tens of new links a second, where the hardest problem is generating codes that never collide, which I solve with a Key Generation Service handing out counter ranges that I base62-encode, unique by construction with no per-write check. The two halves connect through an async Kafka pipeline that counts clicks off the hot path so analytics never slows a redirect. And every hard decision traces back to one principle: this is a read-heavy system, so spend the budget on a fast, always-up redirect and keep the write path simple and collision-proof."
